introSection: Hornet is a steel roller coaster located at Wonderland Amusement Park in Amarillo, Texas, USA. It is a custom installation using Vekoma's MK-700 track system.

sections.title: History
sections.content:
The ride opened in 1988 at Boblo Island in Amherstburg, Ontario, Canada. The ride was called Nightmare and was enclosed in a building shared with the park's dance hall. It closed with the park on 30 September 1993.

In 1994, the ride was sold at auction to Six Flags for $350,000 and was dismantled and relocated to Six Flags AstroWorld in Houston, Texas, USA where the ride reopened as Mayan Mindbender on May 13, 1995. It was reconstructed within a new building designed as a faux-Mayan pyramid. It replaced the miniature golf course in the park's Oriental Village section. The ride had extensive theming, including themed Mayan heads and a crashed jeep in a jungle environment with vegetation.

In 2002, Mayan Mindbender's train was repainted.

In 2003, Mayan Mindbender had seatbelts installed.

The ride operated until the park closed on October 30, 2005.

By January 2006, the ride had been removed from its building and was sold at auction to Wonderland Amusement Park. In late February 2006, the ride's building was removed during the park's demolition. By March 2006, it was transported to Amarillo, Texas. Parts of the ride's Mayan temple building exterior, including the theming, were auctioned off in February 2006 to a dive park in Clute, Texas where they were lowered underground as part of a "virtual underwater theme park".

The ride sat in storage at the park from 2006 to 2007 as the park awaited land to be purchased to build the ride on.

The roller coaster rebuilt at the park in 2008 and reopened on June 3, 2009 as Hornet. Wonderland Amusement Park was the first location that the roller coaster operated outdoors.

sections.title: Color scheme
sections.content: Dark green track and white supports. The ride used to feature brown track and black supports at Six Flags AstroWorld and grey track and supports at Boblo Island.

sections.title: Incident
sections.content: In Summer 2001, a 13-year-old boy was seriously injured when he fell from Mayan Mindbender.

fullText:
Hornet is a steel roller coaster located at Wonderland Amusement Park in Amarillo, Texas, USA. It is a custom installation using Vekoma's MK-700 track system.

The ride opened in 1988 at Boblo Island in Amherstburg, Ontario, Canada. The ride was called Nightmare and was enclosed in a building shared with the park's dance hall. It closed with the park on 30 September 1993.

In 1994, the ride was sold at auction to Six Flags for $350,000 and was dismantled and relocated to Six Flags AstroWorld in Houston, Texas, USA where the ride reopened as Mayan Mindbender on May 13, 1995. It was reconstructed within a new building designed as a faux-Mayan pyramid. It replaced the miniature golf course in the park's Oriental Village section. The ride had extensive theming, including themed Mayan heads and a crashed jeep in a jungle environment with vegetation.

In 2002, Mayan Mindbender's train was repainted.

In 2003, Mayan Mindbender had seatbelts installed.

The ride operated until the park closed on October 30, 2005.

By January 2006, the ride had been removed from its building and was sold at auction to Wonderland Amusement Park. In late February 2006, the ride's building was removed during the park's demolition. By March 2006, it was transported to Amarillo, Texas. Parts of the ride's Mayan temple building exterior, including the theming, were auctioned off in February 2006 to a dive park in Clute, Texas where they were lowered underground as part of a "virtual underwater theme park".

The ride sat in storage at the park from 2006 to 2007 as the park awaited land to be purchased to build the ride on.

The roller coaster rebuilt at the park in 2008 and reopened on June 3, 2009 as Hornet. Wonderland Amusement Park was the first location that the roller coaster operated outdoors.



Dark green track and white supports. The ride used to feature brown track and black supports at Six Flags AstroWorld and grey track and supports at Boblo Island.



In Summer 2001, a 13-year-old boy was seriously injured when he fell from Mayan Mindbender.
